title: Connection Refused by Server
shortDescription: The browser's connection attempt was actively refused by the target server.
fullDescription: ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED occurs when the browser tries to connect to a server but the server actively refuses the connection. This means the server received the connection request but has no service listening on the requested port. It can also indicate a firewall blocking the connection or the website's server being down.

causes.cause: Website server is down or not running
Firewall or antivirus blocking the connection
Incorrect proxy settings configured in the browser or OS
Browser extension interfering with connection
Server port is blocked by ISP or network administrator

symptoms: Browser shows 'This site can't be reached' with ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED
Error occurs for a specific website but others load normally
Error occurs across all websites simultaneously
Site works in one browser but not another

fixSteps.title: Verify the website is up
fixSteps.instruction: Try accessing the website from a different device or use an online service like downforeveryoneorjustme.com to check if the site is down for everyone.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache and cookies
fixSteps.instruction: Open browser settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Select 'Cached images and files' and 'Cookies and other site data'. Restart the browser.

fixSteps.title: Disable proxy settings
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Windows Settings > Network & Internet > Proxy. Turn off 'Use a proxy server'. Also check browser-specific proxy settings in advanced network configuration.

fixSteps.title: Temporarily disable firewall and antivirus
fixSteps.instruction: Disable Windows Firewall or third-party firewall temporarily and try accessing the site again. If it works, add an exception rule for the browser.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists across all browsers and devices on your network, your ISP or network configuration may need professional review.

faq.q: Why does ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED only happen on one website?
faq.a: The specific server you are trying to reach is either down, overloaded, or actively blocking connections from your IP address. Other websites use different servers and are unaffected.

faq.q: Can a VPN fix ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED?
faq.a: Yes, if the server is blocking your IP or your ISP is blocking the site, a VPN can route your traffic through a different path and bypass the restriction.

faq.q: Does ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED mean I have been banned?
faq.a: Not necessarily. While IP bans can cause this error, it is far more commonly caused by the server being down or a local firewall/proxy misconfiguration.

title: Connection Reset During Transfer
shortDescription: The connection to the server was unexpectedly reset during data transfer.
fullDescription: ERR_CONNECTION_RESET occurs when an existing connection between the browser and server is forcibly closed by the remote host. Unlike a refused connection, this error happens after a connection was initially established, meaning the server or an intermediary device dropped the connection mid-transfer. This is often caused by network instability, server overload, or security software interrupting the connection.

causes.cause: Server overloaded and dropping connections
Antivirus or firewall terminating the connection mid-stream
Unstable network connection or packet loss
VPN or proxy server interrupting the connection
Corrupted Winsock catalog or TCP/IP stack

symptoms: Page partially loads then shows ERR_CONNECTION_RESET
Error appears intermittently when loading the same page
Downloads fail partway through with a connection error
Streaming content buffers then disconnects

fixSteps.title: Restart your router and modem
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug your router and modem from power, wait 30 seconds, then plug them back in. Wait for full reconnection before testing.

fixSteps.title: Disable VPN or proxy temporarily
fixSteps.instruction: If you are using a VPN or proxy, disconnect from it and try loading the page directly to see if the intermediary is causing the reset.

fixSteps.title: Reset Winsock and TCP/IP stack
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: netsh winsock reset followed by netsh int ip reset. Restart your computer afterward.

fixSteps.title: Check antivirus SSL scanning settings
fixSteps.instruction: Open your antivirus settings and look for HTTPS/SSL scanning or web protection features. Temporarily disable them and test. If the error resolves, add an exception for the affected site.

whenToCallPro: If resetting the network stack does not help and the error occurs on all websites across all devices, contact your ISP to check for line issues.

faq.q: Why does ERR_CONNECTION_RESET happen randomly?
faq.a: Intermittent resets typically indicate network instability such as packet loss, an overloaded router, or a flaky cable or Wi-Fi connection causing drops during data transfer.

faq.q: Is ERR_CONNECTION_RESET a server problem or a client problem?
faq.a: It can be either. If only one website is affected, the server is likely at fault. If multiple websites show the error, the issue is on your end or with your ISP.

faq.q: Can malware cause ERR_CONNECTION_RESET?
faq.a: Yes. Certain malware modifies network settings or injects itself into network traffic, which can cause connection resets. Running a full malware scan is recommended.

title: Connection Timed Out
shortDescription: The browser waited too long for the server to respond and gave up.
fullDescription: ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT occurs when the browser sends a request to a server but receives no response within the allowed time period, typically around 30 seconds. The server may be unreachable, extremely slow, or the request may be blocked somewhere along the network path. Unlike a refused connection, there is no active rejection — the request simply goes unanswered.

causes.cause: Server is overloaded or unresponsive
Slow or congested internet connection
Firewall silently dropping packets instead of rejecting them
DNS resolution delays causing the initial handshake to fail
Incorrect hosts file entries redirecting requests to invalid addresses

symptoms: Browser loading spinner runs for a long time before showing the error
Page eventually displays 'This site can't be reached' with ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT
Some pages on the same site load while others time out
Error is more frequent during peak internet usage hours

fixSteps.title: Check your internet speed
fixSteps.instruction: Run a speed test at speedtest.net or fast.com. If your connection is significantly slower than expected, restart your router or contact your ISP.

fixSteps.title: Flush DNS cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: ipconfig /flushdns. This clears stale DNS entries that might be pointing to unresponsive addresses.

fixSteps.title: Check the hosts file for bad entries
fixSteps.instruction: Open C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts in Notepad (run as administrator). Remove any entries that redirect the affected website to 127.0.0.1 or other incorrect IPs.

fixSteps.title: Change DNS servers
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Network Settings > Change adapter options > right-click your connection > Properties > IPv4 > Use the following DNS: set Preferred to 8.8.8.8 and Alternate to 8.8.4.4 (Google DNS).

whenToCallPro: If the timeout persists on all websites across multiple devices on the same network, your ISP or network infrastructure may need professional attention.

faq.q: Why does ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT happen only on certain sites?
faq.a: Those specific servers may be overloaded, geographically distant with high latency, or experiencing issues. Other sites respond faster and complete the connection in time.

faq.q: Can a slow computer cause connection timeouts?
faq.a: A slow computer alone typically does not cause this error. It is almost always a network or server issue, though extremely high CPU usage could indirectly delay network operations.

faq.q: How can I increase the browser timeout limit?
faq.a: Most browsers do not expose timeout settings directly. In Chrome, you can use extensions to increase timeout thresholds, but the real fix is to address the underlying network or server issue.

title: SSL Protocol Error
shortDescription: The browser could not establish a secure SSL/TLS connection with the server.
fullDescription: ERR_SSL_PROTOCOL_ERROR indicates that the browser failed to negotiate a secure SSL/TLS connection with the web server. The SSL handshake process broke down, preventing encrypted communication from being established. This can result from misconfigured SSL certificates on the server, outdated TLS protocol versions, interference from security software, or incorrect system clock settings on the client machine.

causes.cause: Server SSL certificate is misconfigured or incomplete
System date and time are significantly incorrect
Antivirus or firewall performing SSL inspection and corrupting the handshake
Outdated browser not supporting modern TLS versions
QUIC protocol interference with SSL negotiation

symptoms: Browser shows 'This site can't provide a secure connection' with ERR_SSL_PROTOCOL_ERROR
Error only appears on HTTPS sites, HTTP sites load fine
Same site works in a different browser or device
Error started appearing after a software update or antivirus installation

fixSteps.title: Check system date and time
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the clock in the Windows taskbar and select 'Adjust date/time'. Enable 'Set time automatically' and 'Set time zone automatically'. Click 'Sync now'.

fixSteps.title: Clear SSL state
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options (search in Start menu) > Content tab > click 'Clear SSL state'. Then restart your browser.

fixSteps.title: Disable QUIC protocol in Chrome
fixSteps.instruction: Type chrome://flags in the address bar. Search for 'Experimental QUIC protocol' and set it to Disabled. Relaunch Chrome.

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus SSL scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Open your antivirus program settings and look for SSL/TLS inspection, HTTPS scanning, or encrypted connection scanning. Disable these features and test the website.

whenToCallPro: If the error appears across all HTTPS websites after trying all fixes, your system's SSL/TLS configuration may be corrupted and need professional repair.

faq.q: Is ERR_SSL_PROTOCOL_ERROR dangerous?
faq.a: The error itself is not dangerous — it means the secure connection failed. However, you should not bypass it to access the site without encryption, as your data would be transmitted in plain text.

faq.q: Why did this error start appearing suddenly?
faq.a: Common triggers include a system clock drift, an antivirus update that enabled SSL scanning, a Windows update that changed TLS settings, or the website updating its SSL certificate configuration.

faq.q: Can I fix this if the problem is on the server side?
faq.a: No. If the server's SSL certificate is misconfigured, only the website administrator can fix it. You can try accessing the site later or contact the site owner.

title: SSL Version or Cipher Mismatch
shortDescription: The browser and server could not agree on a compatible SSL/TLS version or encryption cipher.
fullDescription: ERR_SSL_VERSION_OR_CIPHER_MISMATCH occurs when the browser and the web server cannot negotiate a mutually supported SSL/TLS protocol version or encryption cipher suite. Modern browsers have dropped support for older, insecure protocols like SSL 3.0 and TLS 1.0, so servers still using these outdated protocols will trigger this error. It can also occur when the server's certificate uses an unsupported signature algorithm.

causes.cause: Server only supports deprecated TLS versions (TLS 1.0 or 1.1)
Server certificate uses an outdated signature algorithm like SHA-1
Server only offers weak cipher suites that the browser rejects
CDN or load balancer misconfigured with incompatible SSL settings

symptoms: Browser displays 'This site can't provide a secure connection' with cipher mismatch details
Error only affects specific websites, not all HTTPS sites
The same website may work in older browser versions but not current ones
Error page may show specific unsupported protocol or cipher details

fixSteps.title: Update your browser
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure you are running the latest version of your browser. Go to Settings > About (in Chrome, Edge, or Firefox) and install any available updates.

fixSteps.title: Enable all TLS versions in Internet Options
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options > Advanced tab. Under Security, ensure TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 are checked. Uncheck SSL 3.0. Click Apply and OK, then restart the browser.

fixSteps.title: Clear SSL state and browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open Internet Options > Content tab > Clear SSL state. Then clear your browser cache completely and restart the browser.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website administrator
fixSteps.instruction: If none of the above work, the issue is server-side. Contact the website owner and let them know their SSL/TLS configuration needs updating to support modern protocols and ciphers.

whenToCallPro: This error is almost always a server-side issue. If you are a website owner, contact your hosting provider or a web security professional to update your SSL/TLS configuration.

faq.q: Can I force my browser to use older TLS versions?
faq.a: Technically yes through advanced settings, but this is strongly discouraged as it exposes you to known security vulnerabilities. It is better to wait for the website to update its server configuration.

faq.q: Why does this site work on my phone but not my computer?
faq.a: Different browsers and operating systems support different TLS versions and ciphers. Your phone's browser may still support the older protocol that the server requires.

faq.q: Is this error related to my SSL certificate?
faq.a: If you are a website visitor, the problem is with the server's certificate or configuration, not yours. If you are the site owner, you need to update your server's SSL/TLS settings and possibly reissue the certificate.

title: Certificate Authority Not Trusted
shortDescription: The website's SSL certificate was issued by a certificate authority that is not trusted by the browser.
fullDescription: ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID means the browser does not trust the certificate authority (CA) that signed the website's SSL certificate. This can happen when the certificate is self-signed, issued by an internal corporate CA not in the browser's trust store, expired, or when intermediate certificates are missing from the server configuration. The browser blocks the connection to protect against potential man-in-the-middle attacks.

causes.cause: Website using a self-signed SSL certificate
Server missing intermediate certificates in the chain
System date is incorrect causing valid certificates to appear invalid
Antivirus replacing the site certificate with its own untrusted one
Corporate network using an internal CA not trusted by the browser

symptoms: Browser shows a red 'Not Secure' warning with certificate error details
A full-page warning with 'Your connection is not private' message appears
The padlock icon in the address bar shows an error or warning state
Error appears on sites that previously worked fine with HTTPS

fixSteps.title: Check system date and time
fixSteps.instruction: Verify your system clock is correct. Right-click the taskbar clock > Adjust date/time > enable automatic time. An incorrect date makes valid certificates appear expired or not yet valid.

fixSteps.title: Update root certificates via Windows Update
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Windows Update and install all available updates. Windows Update includes root certificate updates that expand the list of trusted CAs.

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus HTTPS scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Open your antivirus settings and disable HTTPS/SSL scanning features. Some antivirus programs replace site certificates with their own, causing trust errors.

fixSteps.title: Install the missing CA certificate (corporate networks)
fixSteps.instruction: If on a corporate network, ask your IT department for the company's root CA certificate. Install it by double-clicking the .crt file and selecting 'Place in Trusted Root Certification Authorities'.

whenToCallPro: If you are a website owner and visitors report this error, contact your hosting provider to properly configure the SSL certificate chain.

faq.q: Is it safe to bypass the ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID warning?
faq.a: Generally no. The warning exists to protect you from potentially fraudulent sites. Only proceed if you fully trust the site and understand the risk, such as accessing your own development server.

faq.q: Why does my antivirus cause certificate errors?
faq.a: Many antivirus programs intercept HTTPS traffic to scan it for threats. They do this by replacing the site's certificate with their own. If the antivirus root certificate is not properly installed, the browser shows a trust error.

faq.q: Can this error mean someone is intercepting my connection?
faq.a: Yes. A man-in-the-middle attack would present an untrusted certificate. This is exactly what the browser is warning you about. If you see this on a site that normally works, investigate before proceeding.

title: Certificate Date Invalid
shortDescription: The website's SSL certificate has expired or is not yet valid.
fullDescription: ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID occurs when the website's SSL certificate dates do not match the current date. Either the certificate has passed its expiration date and needs renewal, or it is not yet valid because the certificate's start date is in the future. This error can also appear when your computer's clock is set incorrectly, making a valid certificate appear expired or not yet active.

causes.cause: The website's SSL certificate has expired and not been renewed
Your computer's system clock is set to the wrong date or time
CMOS battery is dead causing the system clock to reset on every boot
Certificate was recently renewed but CDN cache still serves the old one

symptoms: Browser shows 'Your connection is not private' with NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID
Certificate details show an expiration date in the past
The error appears on multiple previously working HTTPS sites simultaneously
Error resolves temporarily after fixing the clock but returns after restart

fixSteps.title: Fix your system clock
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the clock on the taskbar > Adjust date/time. Turn on 'Set time automatically' and 'Set time zone automatically'. Click 'Sync now' to sync with a time server.

fixSteps.title: Check the certificate expiration date
fixSteps.instruction: Click the padlock icon in the address bar > Certificate. Check the 'Valid from' and 'Valid to' dates. If the certificate is expired, the website owner needs to renew it.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache and SSL state
fixSteps.instruction: Clear your browser cache and cookies, then open Internet Options > Content > Clear SSL state. Restart the browser to pick up any recently renewed certificates.

fixSteps.title: Replace CMOS battery if clock keeps resetting
fixSteps.instruction: If your system clock resets to an incorrect date every time you restart, the CMOS battery on the motherboard needs replacement. It is a standard CR2032 coin cell battery.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after correcting your system clock and the certificate is genuinely expired, only the website administrator can fix it by renewing their SSL certificate.

faq.q: Why does this error appear on many websites at the same time?
faq.a: When your system clock is wrong, all certificates appear to have invalid dates. If the error affects multiple sites simultaneously, your clock is almost certainly the cause.

faq.q: Why does my clock keep resetting to the wrong date?
faq.a: Your CMOS battery on the motherboard is likely dead. This small battery maintains the system clock when the computer is powered off. Replacing it costs only a few dollars.

faq.q: Should I bypass this warning and access the site anyway?
faq.a: Only if you have verified the issue is with your own clock. If the site's certificate is genuinely expired, proceeding means your connection will not be properly encrypted.

title: DNS Domain Does Not Exist
shortDescription: The DNS server confirmed that the requested domain name does not exist.
fullDescription: DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN means the browser queried the DNS server for the domain name, and the DNS server authoritatively responded that no such domain exists (NXDOMAIN). This can happen when you misspell a URL, the domain has expired and been removed from DNS, or your DNS settings are misconfigured and pointing to a server that cannot resolve the domain properly.

causes.cause: Misspelled URL or domain name
Domain name has expired or been deleted by the owner
DNS server is not resolving correctly due to misconfiguration
Local hosts file is redirecting the domain incorrectly
Browser DNS cache holds a stale NXDOMAIN entry

symptoms: Browser shows 'This site can't be reached' with DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NXDOMAIN
The URL in the address bar may contain a typo
The domain was working recently but suddenly stopped resolving
Other websites load fine but one specific domain fails

fixSteps.title: Double-check the URL spelling
fixSteps.instruction: Carefully review the URL in the address bar for typos. Common mistakes include extra letters, missing letters, or wrong TLD (e.g., .com vs .org vs .net).

fixSteps.title: Flush DNS cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: ipconfig /flushdns. Also clear Chrome's DNS cache by visiting chrome://net-internals/#dns and clicking 'Clear host cache'.

fixSteps.title: Change DNS servers
fixSteps.instruction: Switch to public DNS servers. Go to Network Settings > Change adapter options > right-click connection > Properties > IPv4. Set DNS to 8.8.8.8 (Google) or 1.1.1.1 (Cloudflare).

fixSteps.title: Check the hosts file
fixSteps.instruction: Open C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts in Notepad (as admin). Look for entries that redirect the domain to 0.0.0.0 or 127.0.0.1 and remove them if they should not be there.

whenToCallPro: If changing DNS servers and flushing the cache do not help and you are certain the domain exists, contact your ISP to check if they are blocking the domain at the DNS level.

faq.q: What does NXDOMAIN mean?
faq.a: NXDOMAIN stands for Non-Existent Domain. It is the DNS server's authoritative response indicating that the domain name you queried simply does not exist in DNS records.

faq.q: Can my ISP cause this error?
faq.a: Yes. Some ISPs redirect NXDOMAIN responses to their own search pages or may block certain domains. Switching to a third-party DNS like Google (8.8.8.8) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) can bypass this.

faq.q: How can I check if a domain has expired?
faq.a: Use a WHOIS lookup tool (whois.domaintools.com or similar) to check the domain's registration status and expiration date.

title: DNS Probe Found No Internet Connection
shortDescription: The DNS probe failed because there is no active internet connection.
fullDescription: DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NO_INTERNET indicates that the browser attempted a DNS lookup but could not reach any DNS server because the device has no active internet connection. This is different from NXDOMAIN, which means the DNS responded. In this case, the DNS query itself could not be sent. The cause is typically a disconnected network adapter, router issue, or network configuration problem.

causes.cause: Wi-Fi or Ethernet adapter is disconnected or disabled
Router or modem has lost its internet connection
Network adapter driver issue after a Windows update
DNS server addresses are misconfigured or empty

symptoms: All websites fail to load with DNS_PROBE_FINISHED_NO_INTERNET
Network icon in the taskbar shows 'No Internet' or a yellow warning triangle
Wi-Fi shows connected but no internet access
Other devices on the same network may or may not have internet

fixSteps.title: Check physical network connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify that your Wi-Fi is turned on or your Ethernet cable is securely plugged in. Check the network icon in the taskbar for connection status.

fixSteps.title: Restart router and modem
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug your router and modem from power. Wait 30 seconds. Plug the modem in first, wait for it to fully connect, then plug in the router. Wait 2 minutes before testing.

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Network Diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the network icon in the taskbar and select 'Troubleshoot problems' or 'Diagnose'. Follow the automated troubleshooter's recommendations.

fixSteps.title: Reset network adapter
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Advanced network settings > Network reset. This reinstalls all network adapters and resets networking components to their defaults. Your computer will restart.

whenToCallPro: If none of the devices on your network can access the internet after restarting the router and modem, contact your ISP as the issue may be on their end.

faq.q: Why does it say DNS probe when the real problem is no internet?
faq.a: The browser first tries DNS resolution when loading a page. If there is no internet, the DNS query fails, and the browser reports the failure at the DNS stage since that is where the process stopped.

faq.q: My Wi-Fi says connected but I get this error. Why?
faq.a: Being connected to Wi-Fi only means you are connected to your router. If the router itself has lost its connection to your ISP, you have local network access but no internet.

faq.q: Can a VPN cause this error?
faq.a: Yes. A VPN that disconnects unexpectedly can leave your network in a state where DNS queries are routed to a VPN server that is no longer reachable. Disconnect and reconnect the VPN to fix this.

title: Network Certificate Authority Invalid
shortDescription: The browser's network stack rejected the certificate because its issuing authority is not trusted.
fullDescription: NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID is Chrome's network-level certificate error indicating the SSL certificate presented by the server was signed by a certificate authority that is not in the browser's or operating system's trust store. This is functionally similar to ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID but appears at the network layer. Common causes include self-signed certificates, missing intermediate certificates, and antivirus HTTPS inspection replacing certificates.

causes.cause: Self-signed certificate used on the server
Intermediate certificate chain is incomplete on the server
Antivirus SSL inspection replacing the site certificate
Outdated operating system missing recent root CA updates

symptoms: Full-page 'Your connection is not private' warning with NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID
Advanced details show the certificate issuer as unknown or untrusted
Error affects a specific site while others with HTTPS work normally
Clicking 'Advanced' reveals options to proceed at your own risk

fixSteps.title: Check the certificate issuer
fixSteps.instruction: Click the padlock or warning icon in the address bar, then view the certificate details. Note who issued the certificate — if it says the same name as the site, it is self-signed.

fixSteps.title: Update Windows root certificates
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Update (Settings > Update & Security > Windows Update) to get the latest root certificate updates from Microsoft.

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus HTTPS scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Open your antivirus program and disable HTTPS/SSL scanning. Restart the browser and retest. If the error resolves, add the site as an exception in your antivirus.

fixSteps.title: Manually install the root CA certificate
fixSteps.instruction: If this is a corporate or development environment, obtain the root CA certificate file (.crt or .cer). Double-click it, select 'Install Certificate', choose 'Local Machine', and place it in 'Trusted Root Certification Authorities'.

whenToCallPro: If you are a website owner and visitors see this error, your SSL certificate chain is misconfigured. Contact your hosting provider or a security professional to fix the certificate setup.

faq.q: What is the difference between ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID and NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID?
faq.a: They indicate the same underlying problem. The NET:: prefix indicates the error originated from the browser's network stack layer. The fix steps are identical for both.

faq.q: Can I permanently trust a self-signed certificate?
faq.a: Yes. You can export the certificate from the browser and install it in your system's Trusted Root Certification Authorities store. However, only do this for certificates you fully trust, like your own development server.

faq.q: Does this error mean the website has been hacked?
faq.a: Not necessarily, but it could indicate a man-in-the-middle attack. More commonly it means the website's SSL certificate is misconfigured. If you see this on a well-known site that normally works, be cautious.

title: Network Connection Changed
shortDescription: The network connection changed while a page was loading, interrupting the request.
fullDescription: ERR_NETWORK_CHANGED occurs when the browser detects that the underlying network connection has changed during an active request. This typically happens when switching between Wi-Fi networks, transitioning from Wi-Fi to mobile data, connecting or disconnecting a VPN, or when DHCP renews your IP address. The browser aborts the in-progress request because the network path has changed and the connection is no longer valid.

causes.cause: Switching between Wi-Fi networks or Wi-Fi and Ethernet
VPN connecting or disconnecting during page load
DHCP lease renewal changing the device's IP address
Unstable Wi-Fi causing frequent reconnections

symptoms: Page load interrupted with ERR_NETWORK_CHANGED message
Error coincides with a network switch or VPN state change
Refreshing the page immediately after the error often resolves it
Error occurs frequently in locations with multiple overlapping Wi-Fi networks

fixSteps.title: Refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Simply press F5 or click the refresh button. This error is often transient and the page will load normally on the second attempt after the network stabilizes.

fixSteps.title: Stabilize your network connection
fixSteps.instruction: If you are switching networks, wait for the new connection to fully establish before loading pages. Ensure you are connected to only one network at a time.

fixSteps.title: Disable network auto-switching
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Wi-Fi settings and disable automatic connection for secondary networks. This prevents your device from unexpectedly switching networks.

fixSteps.title: Set a static IP address
fixSteps.instruction: To prevent DHCP-related changes, set a static IP. Go to Network Settings > your connection > IP settings > Manual. Set an IP, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS manually.

whenToCallPro: If this error occurs frequently without any obvious network changes, your network adapter or router may have a hardware issue that requires professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Why does ERR_NETWORK_CHANGED happen when I connect to a VPN?
faq.a: When a VPN connects, it changes your network routing and IP address. Any active browser connections become invalid because they were established on the old network path, triggering this error.

faq.q: Is this error a sign of a security problem?
faq.a: Usually not. It simply means the network path changed. However, if it happens repeatedly without any obvious cause, it could theoretically indicate network tampering, though hardware issues are far more likely.

faq.q: Can I prevent this error when using a VPN?
faq.a: Connect to your VPN before opening the browser or loading pages. Some VPN clients also have a 'seamless reconnect' feature that minimizes disruption during the transition.

title: Internet Connection Lost
shortDescription: The device has lost its connection to the internet entirely.
fullDescription: ERR_INTERNET_DISCONNECTED means the browser has detected that your device is no longer connected to any network. This is a system-level disconnection, meaning neither DNS resolution nor any network communication can occur. The cause is typically a physical disconnection (Wi-Fi dropped, Ethernet cable unplugged), a disabled network adapter, or a router that has gone offline.

causes.cause: Wi-Fi connection dropped or network adapter disabled
Ethernet cable unplugged or damaged
Router or modem powered off or crashed
Airplane mode accidentally enabled
Network adapter driver crashed or corrupted

symptoms: All websites fail with ERR_INTERNET_DISCONNECTED
Taskbar network icon shows disconnected status
Chrome may display the offline dinosaur game
Other internet-dependent applications also stop working

fixSteps.title: Check Wi-Fi and network connections
fixSteps.instruction: Click the network icon in the taskbar. Verify Wi-Fi is turned on and you are connected to a network. If using Ethernet, check that the cable is securely plugged in at both ends.

fixSteps.title: Disable airplane mode
fixSteps.instruction: Open Settings > Network & Internet > Airplane mode. Ensure it is turned off. You can also check the quick settings panel by clicking the network icon in the taskbar.

fixSteps.title: Restart the network adapter
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Advanced network settings > More network adapter options. Right-click your adapter and select Disable, wait 10 seconds, then Enable.

fixSteps.title: Restart router and modem
fixSteps.instruction: Power cycle your networking equipment. Unplug both the router and modem, wait 30 seconds, then reconnect the modem first and the router second. Wait 2 minutes for full initialization.

whenToCallPro: If the network adapter does not respond after restarting and reinstalling drivers, or if your router needs replacement, professional help may be needed.

faq.q: Why does Chrome show a dinosaur game when there is no internet?
faq.a: Chrome includes an offline Easter egg — the T-Rex runner game. It appears automatically when the browser detects no internet connection, giving you something to do while you troubleshoot.

faq.q: My Wi-Fi says connected but I get ERR_INTERNET_DISCONNECTED. Why?
faq.a: This specific error means the OS reports no network connection at all. If your Wi-Fi icon says connected, try disconnecting and reconnecting. The reported status may be cached.

faq.q: Can a Windows update cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Windows updates sometimes reset network adapter drivers or settings. Restarting the adapter or running the network troubleshooter usually resolves post-update disconnection issues.

title: Domain Name Could Not Be Resolved
shortDescription: The browser could not resolve the website's domain name to an IP address.
fullDescription: ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED indicates that the DNS resolution process failed for the requested domain name. The browser could not translate the human-readable domain name into an IP address needed to connect to the server. This can be caused by DNS server issues, network connectivity problems, browser DNS cache corruption, or an incorrectly typed domain name.

causes.cause: DNS server is unreachable or not responding
Misspelled domain name in the URL
Corrupted browser or system DNS cache
Firewall or security software blocking DNS queries

symptoms: Browser shows 'This site can't be reached' with ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED
Some websites load while others fail to resolve
Error resolves temporarily after flushing DNS but returns
nslookup command in Command Prompt also fails for the domain

fixSteps.title: Verify the URL is correct
fixSteps.instruction: Double-check the URL for typos. Try searching for the website name in a search engine and clicking the official link to ensure you have the correct domain.

fixSteps.title: Flush DNS cache
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: ipconfig /flushdns. In Chrome, also visit chrome://net-internals/#dns and click 'Clear host cache'.

fixSteps.title: Switch to a public DNS server
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Network Settings > Change adapter options > right-click your connection > Properties > Internet Protocol Version 4. Set Preferred DNS to 1.1.1.1 and Alternate DNS to 1.0.0.1 (Cloudflare).

fixSteps.title: Reset DNS client service
fixSteps.instruction: Open Services (services.msc), find 'DNS Client', right-click and select Restart. This forces Windows to reinitialize its DNS resolution system.

whenToCallPro: If all DNS resolution fails across all devices on your network after trying different DNS servers, your router or ISP may need professional troubleshooting.

faq.q: What is DNS and why does it need to resolve names?
faq.a: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names like example.com into IP addresses like 93.184.216.34 that computers use. Without DNS resolution, your browser cannot find the server to connect to.

faq.q: Why does this happen on only one computer but not others?
faq.a: Each computer has its own DNS cache and network configuration. One machine may have a corrupted cache, different DNS settings, or a firewall rule that blocks DNS queries.

faq.q: Is ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED the same as NXDOMAIN?
faq.a: Not exactly. NXDOMAIN means the DNS server responded that the domain does not exist. ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED is broader and can mean the DNS server could not be reached at all, or it failed to respond in time.

title: Cache Miss - Confirm Form Resubmission
shortDescription: The browser cannot retrieve a cached version of the page and may need to resubmit form data.
fullDescription: ERR_CACHE_MISS occurs when the browser is asked to redisplay a page that was the result of a form submission (POST request) but no cached copy exists. This commonly happens when you press the back button after submitting a form, or refresh a page that was loaded via a POST request. The browser cannot simply reload the page without re-sending the form data, which could cause duplicate submissions.

causes.cause: Pressing back button after a form submission
Refreshing a page loaded by a POST request
Website not implementing Post-Redirect-Get pattern correctly
Browser cache has been recently cleared

symptoms: Error appears when pressing the browser back button after a form submission
Page shows 'Confirm Form Resubmission' or ERR_CACHE_MISS
Clicking refresh on a checkout or login confirmation page triggers the error
Error does not appear on regular pages, only after form actions

fixSteps.title: Reload the page
fixSteps.instruction: Click the refresh button or press F5. When prompted about form resubmission, click 'Continue' or 'Resend' if you want to resubmit the form data.

fixSteps.title: Navigate directly to the URL
fixSteps.instruction: Instead of using the back button, type the URL directly in the address bar or use a bookmark to navigate to the page you want without resubmitting form data.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Go to browser Settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Select 'Cached images and files'. This can resolve persistent cache miss issues.

fixSteps.title: Disable cache in Developer Tools (for developers)
fixSteps.instruction: Open DevTools (F12), go to the Network tab, and check 'Disable cache'. This helps developers debug cache-related issues. Remember to uncheck it when done.

whenToCallPro: This error is almost never serious enough to require professional help. If a website you manage consistently produces this error, a web developer should implement the Post-Redirect-Get pattern.

faq.q: Will resubmitting the form cause a duplicate transaction?
faq.a: It depends on the website. Well-designed sites use unique transaction IDs to prevent duplicates, but poorly designed ones may process the same form twice. Check your account for duplicate actions.

faq.q: Why do I only see this on certain websites?
faq.a: Websites that properly implement the Post-Redirect-Get pattern redirect you to a new page after form submission, avoiding this issue. Sites that do not follow this pattern are more likely to trigger the error.

faq.q: Is ERR_CACHE_MISS a security risk?
faq.a: No, it is not a security risk. It is the browser protecting you from accidentally resubmitting data. This is actually a safety feature.

title: Too Many Redirects
shortDescription: The page is caught in a redirect loop, bouncing between URLs endlessly.
fullDescription: ERR_TOO_MANY_REDIRECTS occurs when the browser detects that a page is redirecting to another page, which then redirects back to the original or to yet another redirect, forming an infinite loop. The browser stops following redirects after a certain number (typically 20) to prevent infinite looping. This is usually caused by misconfigured server redirects, conflicting redirect rules, or cookies that force repeated redirects.

causes.cause: Conflicting redirect rules in server configuration (e.g., HTTP to HTTPS and back)
Corrupted cookies causing repeated authentication redirects
CDN or reverse proxy misconfigured with circular redirect rules
WordPress or CMS plugin conflict creating redirect loops

symptoms: Browser shows 'This page isn't working' with ERR_TOO_MANY_REDIRECTS
The address bar may briefly flash between different URLs
Error appears on a specific website but others work fine
The error may appear only when logged in or with specific cookies set

fixSteps.title: Clear cookies for the affected site
fixSteps.instruction: Click the padlock icon in the address bar > Cookies > select the site > Remove. Or go to browser Settings > Privacy > Cookies > search for and delete cookies for the specific site.

fixSteps.title: Clear all cookies and cache
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data. Select 'Cookies and other site data' and 'Cached images and files'. Try accessing the site again.

fixSteps.title: Try in incognito or private mode
fixSteps.instruction: Open a new incognito/private window (Ctrl+Shift+N in Chrome). Try accessing the site. If it works, the issue is with your stored cookies or extensions.

fixSteps.title: Check for conflicting browser extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Disable all browser extensions, then try loading the site. If it works, re-enable extensions one by one to find the one causing the redirect loop.

whenToCallPro: If you are the website owner and clearing cookies does not fix the issue for visitors, your server redirect configuration needs professional review.

faq.q: Why does clearing cookies fix redirect loops?
faq.a: Some redirect loops are caused by cookies that trigger authentication or preference redirects. Clearing cookies removes these triggers and lets the site set fresh cookies without the loop.

faq.q: Can ad blockers cause redirect loops?
faq.a: Yes. Ad blockers and privacy extensions can interfere with redirect chains, especially on sites that check for ad blocker status and redirect accordingly.

faq.q: Is this error always the website's fault?
faq.a: Most often yes, but corrupted cookies or interfering browser extensions on your end can also create the loop. Try clearing cookies and testing in incognito mode before blaming the site.

title: Bad Request
shortDescription: The server could not understand the request due to malformed syntax or invalid data.
fullDescription: HTTP 400 Bad Request means the server received a request it could not process because the request itself was malformed. This could be due to corrupted cookies being sent with the request, an excessively large URL, invalid characters in the request headers, or the request body not matching the expected format. The server rejects the request without processing it.

causes.cause: Corrupted or oversized cookies for the website
URL is too long or contains invalid characters
Browser extension modifying the request headers incorrectly
Server-side validation rejecting the submitted data

symptoms: Page displays '400 Bad Request' error message
Error occurs on a specific site but others work normally
Error appears after filling out forms or performing search queries
Error resolves after clearing cookies for the site

fixSteps.title: Clear cookies for the specific site
fixSteps.instruction: Click the padlock icon in the address bar and remove cookies for the affected domain. Alternatively, go to Settings > Privacy > Cookies and delete cookies for the specific site.

fixSteps.title: Check the URL
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the URL does not contain unusual characters, excessive length, or encoding errors. Try navigating to the site's homepage directly instead of using a deep link.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Privacy > Clear browsing data and clear 'Cached images and files'. Stale cached files can cause request mismatches.

fixSteps.title: Try in incognito mode
fixSteps.instruction: Open an incognito/private window and try accessing the URL. If it works, the issue is related to your cookies, cache, or browser extensions.

whenToCallPro: If you consistently get 400 errors on a website you manage, a web developer should inspect server logs to identify what is malformed in the requests.

faq.q: Is a 400 error my fault or the server's fault?
faq.a: It can be either. Corrupted cookies or a malformed URL on your end can cause it, but a server bug that rejects valid requests is also possible.

faq.q: Why does clearing cookies fix 400 errors?
faq.a: Cookies are sent with every request to a website. If cookies become corrupted, excessively large, or contain invalid data, the server may reject the entire request as malformed.

faq.q: Can a 400 error indicate a security issue?
faq.a: Not typically. It usually indicates a data formatting problem. However, some servers return 400 for requests they suspect are malicious, as a form of input validation.

title: Unauthorized - Authentication Required
shortDescription: The server requires authentication credentials that were not provided or are invalid.
fullDescription: HTTP 401 Unauthorized means the request requires user authentication. Either no credentials were provided, or the provided credentials (username, password, API key, or token) were invalid. This is different from 403 Forbidden, which means you are authenticated but not authorized. A 401 typically prompts the browser to display a login dialog or the website shows its own login page.

causes.cause: Login session has expired and needs re-authentication
Incorrect username or password entered
Authentication token or cookie has become invalid
Server authentication configuration changed

symptoms: Page shows '401 Unauthorized' or prompts for login credentials
Previously accessible pages suddenly require login
API calls return 401 after a period of inactivity
Browser shows a basic authentication popup dialog

fixSteps.title: Log in again
fixSteps.instruction: Your session may have expired. Navigate to the site's login page and sign in again with your credentials.

fixSteps.title: Verify your credentials
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure you are using the correct username and password. Try resetting your password if you are unsure. Check for caps lock being on.

fixSteps.title: Clear cookies and site data
fixSteps.instruction: Clear cookies for the specific site to remove any expired or corrupted authentication tokens. Then try logging in again with fresh cookies.

fixSteps.title: Check for URL or bookmark issues
fixSteps.instruction: Verify that the URL you are accessing is correct and you have not bookmarked a protected API endpoint or admin URL that requires special credentials.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot log in despite having correct credentials and password reset does not help, contact the website's support team or your system administrator.

faq.q: What is the difference between 401 and 403?
faq.a: A 401 means you are not authenticated (not logged in or bad credentials). A 403 means you are authenticated but do not have permission to access that specific resource.

faq.q: Why did I get logged out suddenly?
faq.a: Sessions expire after a period of inactivity for security reasons. The timeout varies by website but is commonly 15-60 minutes. Some sites also invalidate sessions when your IP address changes.

faq.q: Can browser extensions cause 401 errors?
faq.a: Yes. Privacy extensions that block cookies or modify request headers can strip authentication tokens from requests, causing the server to see you as unauthenticated.

title: Forbidden - Access Denied
shortDescription: The server understood the request but refuses to authorize access to the resource.
fullDescription: HTTP 403 Forbidden means the server understood your request and knows who you are, but you do not have permission to access the requested resource. Unlike a 401, providing different credentials will not help — your account simply does not have the required access level. This can also occur when the server blocks requests based on IP address, geographic location, or detected suspicious behavior.

causes.cause: User account does not have permission to access the resource
IP address or geographic region blocked by the server
Server directory listing disabled and no index page exists
Web Application Firewall blocking the request as suspicious

symptoms: Page displays '403 Forbidden' or 'Access Denied' message
The error occurs even after logging in with valid credentials
Some pages on the site work but specific sections return 403
Error may include details about which permission is missing

fixSteps.title: Verify your access permissions
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm with the website or service administrator that your account has access to the specific resource. You may need elevated permissions or a different subscription level.

fixSteps.title: Check if the URL is correct
fixSteps.instruction: You may be trying to access an administrative or restricted area by mistake. Navigate to the main page and look for the correct link to your intended destination.

fixSteps.title: Disable VPN and try again
fixSteps.instruction: If you are using a VPN, the server may be blocking the VPN's IP address. Disconnect the VPN and try accessing the site from your regular connection.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser data and try in incognito
fixSteps.instruction: Clear cookies and cache for the site, or try in an incognito window. Sometimes stale session data can cause permission issues after account changes.

whenToCallPro: If you are a website owner and legitimate users are getting 403 errors, review your server's access control configuration, .htaccess rules, and web application firewall settings.

faq.q: Why am I getting 403 when I was able to access this page before?
faq.a: Your permissions may have been changed, the site's access rules may have been updated, or your IP address may have been flagged. Contact the site administrator for clarification.

faq.q: Can clearing cookies fix a 403 error?
faq.a: Sometimes. If the 403 is caused by stale session data, clearing cookies and re-authenticating can resolve it. However, if the server is blocking you by IP or permission level, cookies will not help.

faq.q: Is 403 the same as being banned?
faq.a: Not necessarily. A 403 can simply mean you do not have the right permission level for that page. It could also mean your IP is blocked, but it does not always indicate a ban.

title: Page Not Found
shortDescription: The server could not find the requested page or resource.
fullDescription: HTTP 404 Not Found is the most common HTTP error. It means the server is running and responding but the specific page, file, or resource at the requested URL does not exist. This can happen because the page was deleted, the URL was changed without a redirect, there is a typo in the URL, or a link on another site points to a page that no longer exists.

causes.cause: Page has been deleted or moved without a redirect
Typo in the URL
Outdated link or bookmark pointing to a removed page
Website restructured its URLs without proper redirects

symptoms: Browser displays '404 Not Found' or a custom 'Page Not Found' page
The server responds quickly, unlike timeout or connection errors
Other pages on the same website load normally
Error appears from a specific link or bookmark

fixSteps.title: Check the URL for typos
fixSteps.instruction: Look carefully at the URL in the address bar. Common mistakes include wrong case, missing hyphens, extra slashes, or incorrect file extensions. URLs are case-sensitive on most servers.

fixSteps.title: Navigate from the homepage
fixSteps.instruction: Go to the website's main page and use the site's navigation or search function to find the content you are looking for. The page may exist at a different URL.

fixSteps.title: Search for cached version
fixSteps.instruction: Try searching for the page URL in a search engine. Cached versions or the Wayback Machine (web.archive.org) may have a copy of the content.

fixSteps.title: Contact the website
fixSteps.instruction: If you need the content and cannot find it, contact the website owner through their contact page to ask if the content was moved or removed.

whenToCallPro: If you are a website owner with many 404 errors, hire a web developer to set up proper 301 redirects from old URLs to new ones to preserve search rankings and user experience.

faq.q: Does a 404 error mean the whole website is down?
faq.a: No. A 404 means only the specific page you requested was not found. The server is running and other pages may work fine.

faq.q: Can I fix a 404 error myself?
faq.a: If the error is caused by a typo in the URL, you can fix it by correcting the URL. If the page has been genuinely removed from the server, only the website owner can restore it or set up a redirect.

faq.q: Are 404 errors bad for SEO?
faq.a: For website owners, yes. Many 404 errors signal to search engines that the site is poorly maintained. Implementing 301 redirects for moved content preserves link equity and rankings.

title: Method Not Allowed
shortDescription: The HTTP method used in the request is not supported by the target resource.
fullDescription: HTTP 405 Method Not Allowed means the server recognized the request but the HTTP method used (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) is not supported for the requested URL. For example, trying to POST data to a URL that only accepts GET requests. The server's response includes an Allow header listing which methods are supported for the resource.

causes.cause: Form submission uses the wrong HTTP method for the endpoint
Browser extension or proxy modifying the request method
Server misconfiguration restricting allowed methods
API client using PUT or DELETE on a resource that only allows GET and POST

symptoms: Page shows '405 Method Not Allowed' error
Error appears when submitting forms or clicking action buttons
API calls return 405 when using certain HTTP verbs
The response headers include an 'Allow' field listing supported methods

fixSteps.title: Refresh and try again
fixSteps.instruction: Refresh the page and try the action again. Sometimes a temporary server-side issue or caching problem causes incorrect method routing.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache and cookies
fixSteps.instruction: Stale cached redirects can cause the browser to send the wrong HTTP method. Clear cache and cookies for the site and retry.

fixSteps.title: Disable browser extensions
fixSteps.instruction: Some extensions modify request methods or headers. Disable all extensions temporarily and test. Privacy and security extensions are common culprits.

fixSteps.title: Try a different browser
fixSteps.instruction: Test the same action in a different browser. If it works, the issue is specific to your browser configuration or extensions.

whenToCallPro: If the error is consistent and appears for all users, the website's server configuration needs to be updated by a web developer to allow the required HTTP methods.

faq.q: What are HTTP methods?
faq.a: HTTP methods define the type of action. GET retrieves data, POST submits data, PUT updates data, DELETE removes data. Each server endpoint is configured to accept specific methods.

faq.q: Can I fix a 405 error as a regular user?
faq.a: Usually not, since it is a server-side configuration issue. However, clearing cache, disabling extensions, or trying a different browser can resolve cases where the error is caused by client-side interference.

faq.q: Why does the error say my method is not allowed?
faq.a: The URL you are accessing only accepts certain HTTP methods. For instance, a read-only API endpoint may accept GET but not POST. The server is enforcing its allowed method list.

title: Request Timeout
shortDescription: The server timed out waiting for the client to complete its request.
fullDescription: HTTP 408 Request Timeout means the server was willing to accept the connection but the client (browser) did not complete the request within the time the server was willing to wait. This is different from ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT which is a client-side timeout. With 408, the server actively sends this response to close an idle connection. This can occur during large file uploads, slow connections, or when the browser is overwhelmed.

causes.cause: Slow internet connection causing the request to take too long
Large file upload exceeding the server's timeout window
Server configured with very short request timeout limits
Network congestion or packet loss delaying the request transmission

symptoms: Page shows '408 Request Timeout' error message
Error occurs during file uploads or form submissions with large data
The page begins loading but the server sends the timeout before completion
Error is more frequent during peak usage times

fixSteps.title: Retry the request
fixSteps.instruction: Simply try the action again. The timeout may have been caused by a momentary network slowdown. Refreshing the page and resubmitting often works.

fixSteps.title: Check your internet speed
fixSteps.instruction: Run a speed test at speedtest.net. If your upload speed is very slow, consider waiting for a less congested time or connecting via Ethernet instead of Wi-Fi.

fixSteps.title: Reduce upload file size
fixSteps.instruction: If uploading files, try compressing them or splitting them into smaller parts. Large uploads are more likely to exceed the server's timeout window.

fixSteps.title: Close other bandwidth-consuming applications
fixSteps.instruction: Close any downloads, streaming services, or other applications using bandwidth. This frees up your connection for the browser request to complete faster.

whenToCallPro: If you are a website owner and users frequently encounter 408 errors, your server's timeout configuration may need to be increased by a system administrator.

faq.q: What is the difference between HTTP 408 and ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT?
faq.a: HTTP 408 is the server telling you it gave up waiting for your complete request. ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT is your browser giving up waiting for any response from the server. One is server-initiated, the other is client-initiated.

faq.q: Can I increase the server timeout?
faq.a: As a user, no. The server's timeout is configured by the website administrator. As a site owner, you can increase the timeout in your server configuration (e.g., Nginx, Apache, or your hosting panel).

faq.q: Why does this happen more on mobile devices?
faq.a: Mobile connections are often slower and less stable than wired connections, making it more likely that the request takes longer than the server's timeout allows.

title: Too Many Requests - Rate Limited
shortDescription: You have sent too many requests in a short period and have been rate limited.
fullDescription: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests means the server is applying rate limiting because you (or your IP address) have sent too many requests in a given timeframe. Rate limiting is a server protection mechanism to prevent abuse, scraping, or accidental denial-of-service. The response typically includes a Retry-After header indicating how long to wait before trying again.

causes.cause: Refreshing or requesting pages too rapidly
Automated script or browser extension making excessive requests
Multiple users behind the same IP address (corporate network or VPN) triggering shared limits
API key or account has exceeded its rate limit quota

symptoms: Page displays '429 Too Many Requests' or 'Rate limit exceeded' message
Access is temporarily blocked but returns after waiting
Error may include a 'Retry-After' header specifying the wait time
Automated tools or scripts suddenly stop working

fixSteps.title: Wait and retry
fixSteps.instruction: Stop making requests and wait at least 60 seconds before trying again. If the response includes a 'Retry-After' header, wait for the specified duration.

fixSteps.title: Reduce request frequency
fixSteps.instruction: Slow down your browsing or interaction rate with the site. Avoid rapidly clicking links or refreshing pages repeatedly.

fixSteps.title: Check for excessive background requests
fixSteps.instruction: Open DevTools (F12) > Network tab to see if browser extensions or scripts are making requests without your knowledge. Disable suspicious extensions.

fixSteps.title: Switch IP address if on a shared network
fixSteps.instruction: If on a VPN or corporate network, try disconnecting the VPN or using a different network. The rate limit may be applied to the shared IP address used by many users.

whenToCallPro: If you are a developer whose API integration is hitting rate limits, you need to implement proper rate limiting and retry logic in your application code.

faq.q: How long do I need to wait after a 429 error?
faq.a: It varies by server. Check the response for a 'Retry-After' header. If none is provided, start with 60 seconds and increase the wait time if it persists. Some rate limits reset hourly.

faq.q: Can a 429 error lead to a permanent ban?
faq.a: Repeatedly exceeding rate limits can lead to longer blocks or even permanent IP bans on some services. Always respect rate limits to avoid escalation.

faq.q: Why am I rate limited when I have not done anything unusual?
faq.a: If you are behind a shared IP (VPN, corporate network, university), other users sharing that IP may have triggered the limit. Browser extensions can also make hidden background requests.

title: Internal Server Error
shortDescription: The server encountered an unexpected condition that prevented it from processing the request.
fullDescription: HTTP 500 Internal Server Error is a generic server-side error indicating that something went wrong on the server but it cannot provide more specific details. This could be caused by a programming bug, database connection failure, server misconfiguration, or an overwhelmed application. The error is entirely on the server side and there is nothing wrong with your browser or request.

causes.cause: Server-side application code has a bug or unhandled exception
Database server is down or connection pool is exhausted
Server running out of memory or disk space
Corrupted .htaccess file or server configuration error

symptoms: Page displays '500 Internal Server Error' message
Error may appear intermittently, working on some attempts
Error affects all or most pages on the website simultaneously
The error response is fast, unlike timeout-related errors

fixSteps.title: Refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Press F5 or click the refresh button. The error may be transient and resolves on the next request if the server recovers from a momentary issue.

fixSteps.title: Wait and try again later
fixSteps.instruction: If the error persists, wait 5-10 minutes and try again. The server may be overloaded or restarting, and the issue may resolve itself.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Clear your browser cache and cookies for the site. Occasionally a cached redirect or stale session token can trigger server-side errors.

fixSteps.title: Check server status
fixSteps.instruction: Search for '[website name] status' or check the service's official status page or social media accounts for any reported outages.

whenToCallPro: If you are the website owner, a developer needs to check server error logs to identify and fix the root cause of the 500 error.

faq.q: Is a 500 error my fault?
faq.a: No. A 500 error is entirely a server-side problem. There is nothing wrong with your browser, computer, or internet connection.

faq.q: Why does the same page sometimes work and sometimes show 500?
faq.a: Intermittent 500 errors often indicate a server that is occasionally overloaded, has an intermittent database issue, or has a bug that only triggers under certain conditions like high traffic.

faq.q: Can I do anything to fix a 500 error as a visitor?
faq.a: As a visitor, you can try refreshing, clearing cache, or waiting. The actual fix must come from the website's server administrator.

title: Bad Gateway
shortDescription: A gateway or proxy server received an invalid response from the upstream server.
fullDescription: HTTP 502 Bad Gateway means an intermediary server (reverse proxy, load balancer, or CDN) tried to forward your request to the actual backend server but received an invalid or no response. The intermediary is working correctly but the backend server it depends on is not. This is common in architectures where services like Nginx, Cloudflare, or AWS load balancers sit in front of application servers.

causes.cause: Backend application server crashed or is not running
Upstream server is overloaded and not responding to the proxy
Network issue between the proxy or CDN and the origin server
Incompatible protocol response from the backend to the proxy

symptoms: Page shows '502 Bad Gateway' error, often with the proxy server name (Nginx, Cloudflare)
Error appears suddenly on a site that was working moments ago
Different pages on the same site may or may not work
Error page design looks different from the site's normal error page

fixSteps.title: Refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Press F5 or Ctrl+Shift+R to do a hard refresh. The backend server may have recovered from a momentary issue.

fixSteps.title: Wait a few minutes and try again
fixSteps.instruction: 502 errors are typically temporary. Wait 2-5 minutes and try again. The server may be restarting or recovering from a traffic spike.

fixSteps.title: Try a different browser or incognito mode
fixSteps.instruction: Open an incognito window or different browser to rule out caching or extension issues that might affect how the request is routed.

fixSteps.title: Check the site's status page
fixSteps.instruction: Look for an official status page (usually status.sitename.com) or check social media for reported outages. Many services have status dashboards.

whenToCallPro: If you are the site owner, check your proxy/load balancer logs and ensure the backend application server is running and accepting connections.

faq.q: What is a gateway in '502 Bad Gateway'?
faq.a: A gateway is an intermediary server such as a reverse proxy (Nginx), load balancer (AWS ELB), or CDN (Cloudflare) that sits between your browser and the actual web application server.

faq.q: Why does the error page mention Nginx or Cloudflare?
faq.a: Because the intermediary server (Nginx or Cloudflare) is the one that received the bad response and generated the error page. The actual problem is with the backend server behind it.

faq.q: Is a 502 error the same as the site being down?
faq.a: Not exactly. The front-facing server is still running, which is why you get a response at all. The backend application server behind it is having problems.

title: Service Unavailable
shortDescription: The server is temporarily unable to handle the request due to maintenance or overload.
fullDescription: HTTP 503 Service Unavailable means the server is temporarily unable to handle the request. This is typically a temporary condition caused by server maintenance, planned downtime, or the server being overloaded with too many concurrent requests. Unlike 500 errors, a 503 explicitly indicates a temporary state and the server may include a Retry-After header suggesting when to try again.

causes.cause: Server is undergoing scheduled maintenance
Server is overwhelmed by high traffic volume
Server application pool has crashed and is restarting
Hosting provider experiencing infrastructure issues

symptoms: Page shows '503 Service Unavailable' or 'Service Temporarily Unavailable'
Error often includes a message about maintenance or high traffic
The error may include a 'Retry-After' header or estimated return time
All pages on the site are affected simultaneously

fixSteps.title: Wait and refresh
fixSteps.instruction: 503 errors are temporary by definition. Wait 1-5 minutes and refresh the page. If a 'Retry-After' time is given, wait for that duration.

fixSteps.title: Check for maintenance announcements
fixSteps.instruction: Visit the site's status page, blog, or social media accounts for any announced maintenance windows or outage reports.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache
fixSteps.instruction: Clear your browser cache to ensure you are not loading a cached version of the error page. Sometimes the 503 response gets cached even after the server recovers.

fixSteps.title: Try an alternative access method
fixSteps.instruction: If the website has a mobile app, try using that instead. Alternatively, try accessing from a different network to rule out regional or CDN-specific issues.

whenToCallPro: If you own the site, a system administrator should investigate server capacity, auto-scaling configuration, and application health monitoring to prevent future 503 errors.

faq.q: How long does a 503 error usually last?
faq.a: It depends on the cause. Scheduled maintenance typically has a defined window (minutes to hours). Overload-related 503s may resolve within minutes as traffic decreases.

faq.q: Can I cause a 503 error by visiting a site too much?
faq.a: Individually, no. However, if many users visit simultaneously (such as during a product launch), the collective traffic can overwhelm the server and cause 503 for everyone.

faq.q: Is 503 worse than 500?
faq.a: Not necessarily. A 503 explicitly signals a temporary condition, while 500 is a generic error that could be permanent. In some ways, a 503 is more informative and optimistic.

title: Gateway Timeout
shortDescription: A gateway or proxy server did not receive a timely response from the upstream server.
fullDescription: HTTP 504 Gateway Timeout is similar to 502 but specifically indicates a timeout rather than an invalid response. A proxy, load balancer, or CDN waited for a response from the backend server but did not receive one within the configured time limit. The backend server may be processing a complex request, may be overloaded, or may be completely unresponsive.

causes.cause: Backend server is taking too long to process the request
Backend server is down and not responding at all
Network latency between the proxy and the backend server
Proxy server timeout is set too low for complex operations

symptoms: Page shows '504 Gateway Timeout' after a long loading wait
Error commonly appears on pages that perform complex operations (reports, searches)
Simpler pages on the same site may load fine
Error appears more frequently during high-traffic periods

fixSteps.title: Refresh and try again
fixSteps.instruction: Press F5 to refresh. The backend server may have been temporarily overwhelmed and can now respond to your request.

fixSteps.title: Wait a few minutes
fixSteps.instruction: If the server is under heavy load, waiting 2-5 minutes may give it time to clear its queue and respond to new requests.

fixSteps.title: Simplify your request
fixSteps.instruction: If you were performing a complex search, generating a large report, or uploading a big file, try a simpler or smaller request to see if the server can handle that.

fixSteps.title: Check the site status
fixSteps.instruction: Search for the website's status page or check social media for outage reports. If the backend is completely down, you will need to wait for the site operators to fix it.

whenToCallPro: If you are the site owner, investigate backend server performance, increase proxy timeout values, and consider adding caching or scaling up infrastructure.

faq.q: What is the difference between 502 and 504?
faq.a: 502 means the gateway received an invalid response from the backend. 504 means the gateway received no response at all before its timeout expired. Both indicate backend server issues.

faq.q: Can slow internet cause a 504 error?
faq.a: Typically no. The timeout is between the proxy server and the backend server, not between your browser and the proxy. Your internet speed does not directly affect this timeout.

faq.q: Why do complex pages cause 504 more often?
faq.a: Complex operations like database queries, report generation, or large file processing take more server time. If they exceed the proxy's timeout threshold, you get a 504 while simpler pages complete in time.

title: QUIC Protocol Error
shortDescription: An error occurred in the QUIC transport protocol used for HTTP/3 connections.
fullDescription: ERR_QUIC_PROTOCOL_ERROR occurs when there is an issue with the QUIC (Quick UDP Internet Connections) protocol, which is the transport layer for HTTP/3. QUIC uses UDP instead of TCP and provides built-in encryption and multiplexing. This error can be caused by network equipment that blocks or mangles UDP traffic, firewall rules that interfere with QUIC, or bugs in the QUIC implementation on the server or client.

causes.cause: Network firewall or middlebox blocking UDP port 443
Network equipment mangling or dropping QUIC packets
Server-side QUIC implementation bug
Antivirus or security software interfering with QUIC connections

symptoms: Pages fail to load with ERR_QUIC_PROTOCOL_ERROR in the browser
Connections work when QUIC is disabled but fail when enabled
Error occurs on specific networks but not others (e.g., corporate vs home)
Google services and other QUIC-enabled sites are particularly affected

fixSteps.title: Disable QUIC protocol in Chrome
fixSteps.instruction: Navigate to chrome://flags in the address bar. Search for 'Experimental QUIC protocol' and set it to Disabled. Relaunch Chrome. This forces the browser to use TCP/TLS instead.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache and DNS
fixSteps.instruction: Clear browser cache and flush DNS (ipconfig /flushdns in Command Prompt as admin). This removes any cached QUIC connection state.

fixSteps.title: Check firewall UDP rules
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure your firewall allows outbound UDP traffic on port 443. QUIC uses UDP instead of TCP, and some firewalls block UDP traffic by default or on non-standard ports.

fixSteps.title: Update network adapter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Device Manager > Network adapters > right-click your adapter > Update driver. Outdated drivers may not handle QUIC/UDP traffic correctly.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists on a corporate network, the network administrator may need to configure the firewall and network equipment to properly handle QUIC/UDP traffic on port 443.

faq.q: What is QUIC and why is Chrome using it?
faq.a: QUIC is a modern transport protocol developed by Google that uses UDP instead of TCP. It provides faster connections, better multiplexing, and built-in encryption. Chrome uses it by default for supported sites.

faq.q: Is it safe to disable QUIC?
faq.a: Yes. Disabling QUIC simply makes the browser use the traditional TCP/TLS connection method, which is equally secure and functional. You may experience slightly higher latency on some sites.

faq.q: Why does this only happen on certain networks?
faq.a: Some networks, especially corporate and university networks, block UDP traffic or use network equipment that cannot properly handle the QUIC protocol, while home networks typically pass UDP through without issue.

title: HTTP/2 Protocol Error
shortDescription: An error occurred in the HTTP/2 protocol communication between the browser and server.
fullDescription: ERR_HTTP2_PROTOCOL_ERROR indicates that the HTTP/2 connection between the browser and server encountered a protocol-level error. HTTP/2 uses multiplexed streams over a single TCP connection, and if any stream or frame violates the protocol specification, the entire connection may be terminated. Common causes include buggy HTTP/2 server implementations, proxy interference, and network equipment that does not properly support HTTP/2.

causes.cause: Server-side HTTP/2 implementation has a bug or incompatibility
Reverse proxy or CDN incorrectly handling HTTP/2 frames
Antivirus or firewall interfering with HTTP/2 stream multiplexing
Network middlebox corrupting HTTP/2 binary framing

symptoms: Pages fail or partially load with ERR_HTTP2_PROTOCOL_ERROR
Error occurs on some resources within a page while others load
Large file downloads or streaming content frequently triggers the error
The error may appear intermittently rather than consistently

fixSteps.title: Hard refresh the page
fixSteps.instruction: Press Ctrl+Shift+R to force a hard refresh, bypassing the cache. This resets the HTTP/2 connection and can clear transient protocol errors.

fixSteps.title: Clear browser cache and socket pools
fixSteps.instruction: Clear browser cache. In Chrome, also visit chrome://net-internals/#sockets and click 'Flush socket pools' to reset all HTTP/2 connections.

fixSteps.title: Disable antivirus HTTPS scanning
fixSteps.instruction: Antivirus programs that perform HTTPS/SSL scanning can interfere with HTTP/2 binary framing. Disable this feature in your antivirus and test again.

fixSteps.title: Try disabling HTTP/2 via Chrome flags (temporary)
fixSteps.instruction: Visit chrome://flags and search for 'HTTP/2'. If there is an option to disable it, set it to Disabled and relaunch. This forces HTTP/1.1 as a workaround.

whenToCallPro: If you are the website owner and users are reporting HTTP/2 errors, review your web server and reverse proxy HTTP/2 configuration. Consider testing with curl or nghttp2 tools.

faq.q: What is HTTP/2?
faq.a: HTTP/2 is a major revision of the HTTP protocol that enables multiplexed streams, header compression, and server push over a single TCP connection, making web pages load significantly faster than HTTP/1.1.

faq.q: Will disabling HTTP/2 slow down my browsing?
faq.a: Slightly, yes. HTTP/2 is faster than HTTP/1.1 for most websites. However, using it as a temporary workaround while the server issue is fixed is a reasonable trade-off.

faq.q: Is this error related to my internet connection?
faq.a: Usually not directly. The error is about the protocol-level communication failing, which is more commonly caused by server software issues or security software interference than by connection quality.

title: Request Blocked by Client
shortDescription: The request was blocked by a browser extension, ad blocker, or client-side security setting.
fullDescription: ERR_BLOCKED_BY_CLIENT occurs when the browser itself or a browser extension prevents a network request from being sent. This most commonly happens when an ad blocker (such as uBlock Origin or AdBlock) blocks requests to advertising or tracking domains. It can also be caused by privacy extensions, corporate policy extensions, or the browser's built-in tracking protection features.

causes.cause: Ad blocker extension blocking the request
Privacy extension (Privacy Badger, Ghostery) blocking tracking requests
